Aerospace & Defense: Builds aircraft, defense systems, and provides government contracting. Multi-year backlogs provide visibility. Key risks are program delays, government budget changes, and commercial aviation cycles. High R&D and engineering costs.
Airlines: Flies passengers and cargo. Revenue is seats times fare (RASM). Mostly fixed costs with fuel at 30%, labor at 25%, and aircraft leases. Massive operating leverage where a few points of load factor swing profits enormously. Highly cyclical.
Asset Management: Manages money for institutions and individuals, charging a percentage of AUM. Revenue rises and falls with market levels. Very capital-light. Key risks are market declines, fee compression, and the shift to passive investing.
Autos & Parts: Manufactures vehicles and components. Revenue is units times ASP. Extremely capital-intensive with factories, tooling, and R&D. Key risks are economic cycles, interest rate sensitivity, EV transition capex, and tariffs. Highly cyclical.
Banks: Takes deposits (cheap funding) and makes loans (higher rate). Revenue is the net interest spread plus fees. Key risks are credit losses, interest rate risk, and regulatory capital requirements. Low capital intensity but capital-constrained by regulation.
Biotechnology: Develops drugs using biological processes, often targeting rare or complex diseases. Many are pre-revenue, burning cash on clinical trials. Binary outcomes where a single FDA approval can transform the company. Key risks are trial failures, cash burn, dilution, and single-product concentration.
Building Materials: Produces aggregates, cement, lumber, packaging, and paper. Commodity-adjacent but with local transportation cost moats because aggregates are heavy. Key risks are construction cycles, energy costs, and weather. High capital intensity.
Building Products: Manufactures HVAC, windows, doors, roofing, and building components. Tied to housing starts and renovation cycles. More differentiated than building materials with stronger pricing power. Key risks are housing downturns and raw material costs. Medium capital intensity.
Cable & Media: Creates and distributes content including streaming, gaming, broadcasting, publishing, outdoor advertising, and entertainment. Revenue mixes subscriptions, advertising, and licensing. Content costs are semi-fixed. Key risks are cord-cutting, content cost inflation, and streaming competition.
Commercial REITs: Owns office buildings, shopping centers, and net-lease retail properties. Revenue is rent from 5-10 year leases. Net-lease tenants pay all expenses; traditional landlords manage directly. Key risks are work-from-home (office), e-commerce (retail), and tenant bankruptcies.
Diagnostics / CRO: Lab testing companies and contract research organizations running clinical trials for pharma. Revenue is recurring testing fees or multi-year research contracts. Key risks are pharma R&D budget cuts and testing commoditization. Medium capital intensity.
Digital Infrastructure: Owns cell towers, data centers, and fiber networks. Revenue is 5-15 year contracts with carriers and enterprises. Massive upfront capex but tenants almost never leave because switching is extremely disruptive. Key risks are technology shifts and power cost inflation.
Electric Utilities: Generates and distributes electricity. Borrow cheap, build assets, earn a spread between cost of debt and return on assets, repeat for decades. Includes Independent Power Producers, Regulated Power Producers, and Water Utilities. All costs are fixed (power plants, transmission, grid). Key risks are rate case outcomes, fuel costs, extreme weather, and wildfire liability. High capex, very low cyclicality.
Engineering & Construction: Builds infrastructure, power plants, and large-scale projects on contract. Revenue is backlog-driven and lumpy. Costs are labor and materials, mostly variable via subcontractors. Key risks are project delays, cost overruns, and labor shortages. Medium capex, high cyclicality.
Food & Beverage: Manufactures and distributes packaged foods, beverages, and grocery products. Revenue is steady regardless of economy. COGS (ingredients) is 50-60% and variable. Key risks are private label competition, commodity input costs, and shifting health preferences.
General Retail: Big box, home improvement, and discount stores. High volume, lower margins than specialty. COGS is 65-75% of revenue and perfectly variable. Key risks are e-commerce competition and wage inflation. Demand is more resilient than specialty.
Hardware / Networking: Makes physical technology products including computers, servers, networking gear, sensors, and instruments. Revenue driven by enterprise refresh cycles and product launches. Manufacturing and inventory management are key cost drivers.
Healthcare Services: Operates hospitals, outpatient centers, and skilled nursing facilities. Revenue is patient volume times reimbursement rates from insurance/Medicare. Labor-intensive with thin margins. Key risks are reimbursement cuts, labor shortages, and regulatory changes. High capex for facilities.
Homebuilding: Builds and sells residential homes. Revenue is closings x ASP. The land bank (owned entitled lots) is the key asset, tying up capital years before revenue. Key risks are mortgage rate spikes, housing demand cycles, and land impairments. High capital intensity, highly cyclical.
Hotels / Leisure / Gaming: Hotels, casinos, cruise lines, resorts, travel platforms, and entertainment venues. Revenue is occupancy times rate. High fixed costs from property, staff, and maintenance. Key risks are recessions (first discretionary budget cut), travel disruptions, and online competition. Highly cyclical.
Household & Personal: Makes cleaning supplies, personal care, cosmetics, and tobacco. Habitual repeat purchases with strong brand loyalty. Costs are raw materials (variable) and advertising (discretionary). Capital-light manufacturing, very low cyclicality.
IT Services: Consulting, outsourcing, and managed IT services sold on multi-year contracts. Revenue is people times billing rate. Labor is 60-70% of costs. Growth comes from winning contracts and expanding scope within accounts. Capital-light with low cyclicality.
Industrial Distribution: Distributes fasteners, tools, HVAC supplies, and MRO products. Thin margins but high inventory turns. COGS is variable (purchased for resale). Key risks are industrial slowdowns and e-commerce competition. Capital-light, low cyclicality.
Industrial Machinery: Manufactures equipment for factories, farms, construction, and infrastructure. Revenue tracks industrial production and capex cycles. Aftermarket parts and service are higher-margin than equipment sales. Key risks are capex cycle downturns and input cost inflation. Medium capital intensity.
Industrial REITs: Owns warehouses, logistics centers, self-storage, and healthcare facilities. Revenue is rent from long-term leases with built-in escalators. All costs are fixed. Key risks are overbuilding, interest rate increases, and e-commerce maturation.
Insurance: Collects premiums upfront, invests the float, pays claims later. Revenue is premiums plus investment income. Key risks are catastrophe events, investment losses, and reserving errors. Capital-light operationally but capital-constrained by reserve requirements.
Internet / Ad Tech: Digital platforms monetized through advertising, marketplace fees, or transaction cuts. Built once, scaled globally with near-zero marginal cost. Costs are engineering, content moderation, and server infrastructure. Key risks are ad spending cycles, regulatory/antitrust action, and privacy regulation.
Large Pharma: Discovers, develops, and sells branded prescription drugs. Revenue is patent-protected for 10-15 years per drug, then collapses at patent expiry. R&D costs $1-2B per approved drug over 10+ years. Key risks are patent cliffs, drug pricing regulation, and pipeline trial failures.
Managed Care: Sells health insurance plans to employers, individuals, and government programs. Revenue is premiums; costs are medical claims at roughly 85% of revenue. The medical loss ratio is the entire business. Capital-light, low cyclicality.
Medical Devices: Makes surgical instruments, implants, and diagnostic equipment. Razor/blade model where you place the device and sell consumables. Regulatory approval (FDA) creates barriers to entry. Costs are R&D, manufacturing, and a large direct sales force. Medium capital intensity.
Metals & Mining: Mines and processes gold, copper, steel, aluminum, and other metals. Revenue is volume times commodity price with zero pricing power. Costs are volume-driven (mining, energy, processing). Key risks are commodity price collapse, reserve depletion, and environmental/political risk. High capex, highly cyclical.
Oil & Gas E&P: Explores for and produces crude oil and natural gas. Revenue equals production times commodity price with zero pricing power. Costs are drilling (discretionary) and lease operating (semi-fixed). Key risks are commodity price collapse, reserve depletion, and environmental regulation. High capex, extremely cyclical.
Oil & Gas Refining: Buys crude, refines into gasoline/diesel/jet fuel, sells products. Revenue is throughput times crack spread. Crude feedstock is 85-90% of revenue and variable. Key risks are crack spread compression, environmental regulation, and renewable fuel mandates. High capex.
Oil Field Services: Provides drilling rigs, pressure pumping, and well services to E&P companies. Revenue tracks rig count and upstream capex. Key risks are upstream capex cuts (first budget slashed when oil drops) and pricing competition. Medium capex, extremely cyclical.
Payments / Fintech: Processes payments or provides financial technology infrastructure, taking a small cut of every transaction. High volume, thin per-transaction margin, massive scale. Key risks are interchange fee regulation and fintech disruption. Capital-light.
Pipelines / Midstream: Operates pipelines, processing plants, and storage. Revenue is contracted through take-or-pay agreements, not tied to commodity prices. Toll-booth economics. Massive upfront capex but low operating cost. Key risks are production volume declines and permitting barriers.
Railroads: Moves freight by rail. Regulated duopolies with impossible-to-replicate network moats. All costs are fixed (track, locomotives, union labor). Operating ratio is the defining efficiency metric. Key risks are industrial volume declines and regulatory changes. High capex but low cyclicality due to duopoly pricing power.
Regulated Electric: Pure regulated utilities with state-approved rate of return on capital. Revenue and profit set by regulators. Lowest-risk, lowest-growth business model. Same fixed cost base as Electric Utilities but with guaranteed cost recovery.
Renewable Energy: Manufactures solar panels, installs systems, or operates clean energy infrastructure. Revenue mixes equipment sales (lumpy) and power purchase agreements (stable). High upfront build costs. Key risks are subsidy policy changes, technology obsolescence, and project finance interest rates.
Residential REITs: Owns apartments, manufactured housing, and single-family rentals. Revenue is rent times occupancy on 12-month cycles. Property costs are fixed. Key risks are rent control regulation, new supply, and interest rate increases.
Restaurants: Quick-service and full-service dining. Revenue is traffic times check size. Franchise models (MCD, YUM) earn royalties with minimal capex. Company-owned (CMG, DRI) carry rent, labor, and buildout. Key risks are labor costs, food inflation, and consumer spending cuts.
Semiconductors: Designs and/or manufactures chips. Fabless companies design and outsource manufacturing; integrated companies own fabs costing $10B+. Key risks are boom-bust inventory cycles where revenue can swing 20-30% in a quarter, and China/geopolitical exposure.
Software / SaaS: Sells software via subscriptions or licenses with 90%+ retention rates. Costs are R&D headcount and sales teams, capital-light with no physical assets. Revenue is highly recurring and resilient to economic cycles.
Specialty Chemicals: Manufactures specialty chemicals, industrial gases, coatings, and fertilizers. Higher-margin than commodity chemicals from product differentiation and switching costs. COGS (feedstocks) is 40-60% and variable. Key risks are raw material swings, industrial demand cycles, and environmental regulation. Medium capital intensity.
Specialty Retail: Sells apparel, accessories, home goods, and specialty products through stores and online. Costs are inventory (variable), rent and labor (semi-fixed). Key risks are consumer confidence, fashion risk, e-commerce disruption, and markdown pressure. Medium capex, highly cyclical.
Telecom: Provides wireless, wireline, and broadband service. Revenue is subscribers times ARPU. All costs are fixed (network, spectrum, crews). Key risks are churn, price wars, and ongoing capex for 5G/fiber. High capex, low cyclicality.
Trucking & Freight: Moves goods by truck, air, or ocean. Revenue is loads times rate per mile. Asset-based carriers own trucks (high fixed costs). Brokers match loads to carriers (variable, asset-light). Key risks are freight cycles, fuel costs, and driver shortages.
